output:
Using the SaaS Launch Audit skill to produce a full PASS/FAIL report for your B2C fitness app.

---

# Launch Readiness Audit — B2C Consumer Fitness App

**Profile:** B2C · Casual gym-goers 25-40 · Beta → Public Launch · AI workout recs ($0.01/req, 20 req/user/mo) · Freemium + $9.99/mo premium · URL: placeholder

---

## Section 1 — Pre-Launch Validation

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Schedule 10 calls/week for 5 weeks — focus on workout frequency, pain points with
    current apps, and what would make them pay; document verbatim quotes as evidence.

[FAIL] Pre-payment or strong intent secured from early users
  → Next step: Offer beta users a "founding member" deal at $4.99/mo locked forever — real
    payment intent (even a waitlist deposit or pre-order) is the only true validation signal.

[FAIL] Problem validated beyond stated interest
  → Next step: Identify whether beta users return week-over-week without prompting; track 
    D7 and D30 retention — if < 20% return after week 1, the problem isn't painful enough yet.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
  → Next step: Run a "Wizard of Oz" week — deliver AI-generated workout plans manually via 
    DM to 10 beta users before trusting the automated system is solving the real problem.
```

**Section score: 0/4** — This is the highest-risk section. Do not push to public launch until D7 retention is confirmed.

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

```
[PASS] Pricing model selected
  → Freemium + flat subscription is the correct model for B2C fitness (validated by Strava,
    MyFitnessPal, Whoop). Not a per-seat concern here — well chosen.

[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified and communicated
  → Next step: Define exactly what the premium tier unlocks in outcome terms — not "unlimited
    workouts" but "personalized progressive overload plans that adapt weekly." The outcome,
    not the feature, is the value metric.

[FAIL] 3-4 tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: Add at minimum a middle tier — consider Free / Premium $9.99 / Coach $19.99
    (adds form check AI or live coaching access); two-tier freemium leaves money on the table
    from power users willing to pay more.

[FAIL] Annual plan with discount offered
  → Next step: Add annual billing at $79.99/yr ($6.67/mo, ~33% discount) — this dramatically
    improves cash flow and reduces churn; implement before launch, not after.

[FAIL] Pricing page live and scannable in under 5 seconds
  → Next step: Build the pricing page now — it must be live before any social or PH traffic
    hits; include feature comparison table, FAQ, and a clear free vs premium boundary.
```

**Section score: 1/5**

---

## Section 3 — Payment Integration

```
[FAIL] Stripe account activated with live keys
  → Next step: Submit Stripe business verification documents today — approval takes 1-3 days
    and is a hard blocker for everything downstream.

[FAIL] Test transactions completed end-to-end
  → Next step: Run a full test suite: successful charge, failed card (use 4000000000000002),
    upgrade, downgrade, and cancellation — all must work before launch.

[FAIL] Webhook endpoints configured and verified
  → Next step: Register handlers for invoice.payment_succeeded, invoice.payment_failed,
    customer.subscription.deleted, and customer.subscription.updated — missing any of these
    causes silent billing failures at scale.

[FAIL] Customer billing portal accessible
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Customer Portal and link it from your app's account settings page
    — users must be able to self-manage subscriptions or you will drown in support tickets.

[FAIL] Tax and compliance requirements met
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Tax for automatic sales tax collection (US states + EU VAT) —
    B2C at scale without automated tax collection is a legal liability.
```

**Section score: 0/5** — Hard blocker. No launch without live Stripe + webhooks.

---

## Section 4 — AI Economics

```
[PASS] Inference costs calculated per user
  → Confirmed: $0.01/request × 20 requests/month = $0.20/user/month AI cost. Well-modeled.

[PASS] AI costs < 30% of revenue
  → $0.20 / $9.99 = 2.0% of premium revenue. Excellent unit economics — leaves significant
    headroom for infra, support, and margin.

[PASS] Gross margin > 50% on AI component
  → AI cost alone yields ~98% gross margin on the AI layer. Even with 20-30% added for 
    hosting, auth, and storage, you're comfortably in the 65-80% range. Healthy.

[FAIL] Margin protection strategy for heavy users
  → Next step: Cap free-tier users at 5 AI requests/month (not unlimited); for premium, 
    add a soft cap at 50 requests/month with a throttle — a single power user at 200+ 
    requests/month costs $2+ and erases most margin.

[FAIL] AI cost monitoring and alerts configured
  → Next step: Set a Stripe billing alert + an LLM provider spend alert at 110% of projected
    monthly cost — without this, a viral moment or abuse pattern will be invisible until 
    your invoice arrives.
```

**Section score: 3/5** — Strongest section. Fix the cap and monitoring before launch.

---

## Section 5 — GEO & AI Discovery

```
[FAIL] AI visibility audit completed
  → Next step: Manually query ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude with "best AI fitness apps 2026"
    and "workout recommendation apps" — document whether your app appears; if not, this is your
    baseline to improve from post-launch.

[FAIL] Structured data / FAQ schema implemented
  → Next step: Add FAQ schema markup to your homepage and pricing page answering "What does
    [AppName] do?", "How does AI personalize workouts?", and "Is [AppName] free?" — this is
    the primary signal LLM crawlers use to cite you.

[FAIL] Content uses authoritative, citable language
  → Next step: Audit your homepage copy — replace hedged phrases like "may help you improve"
    with specific, citable claims like "users complete 3.2x more workouts in month one";
    AI citation engines favor declarative, evidence-backed language.

[FAIL] SSR or pre-rendering confirmed for LLM crawlers
  → Next step: Verify your app is server-side rendered or uses static generation for all
    public-facing pages — SPAs that rely on client-side JS are invisible to AI crawlers.

[FAIL] Server response time < 200ms
  → Next step: Cannot verify (no live URL) — before launch, run Lighthouse and confirm
    TTFB < 200ms; use a CDN for static assets if you are not already.

[FAIL] Core Web Vitals passing (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms)
  → Next step: Cannot verify — run PageSpeed Insights on your landing page before launch;
    failing Core Web Vitals directly impacts both SEO rank and AI citation likelihood.
```

**Section score: 0/6** — B2C fitness is a crowded AI-search category. GEO setup is critical before launch to capture the 33% of organic traffic coming from AI agents.

---

## Section 6 — Competitor Intelligence

```
[FAIL] 3+ direct competitors identified
  → Next step: Your primary competitors are Freeletics, Future (AI coaching), Fitbod, and
    the AI tier of MyFitnessPal — document each one's AI feature set, pricing, and user reviews
    before positioning your launch.

[FAIL] Competitor pricing documented
  → Next step: Fitbod is $14.99/mo, Future is $199/mo (live coaching), Freeletics is $9.99/mo,
    MyFitnessPal Premium is $19.99/mo — your $9.99 is competitive but you need this documented
    to defend it in copy, ads, and press.

[FAIL] Differentiation statement written
  → Next step: Write one sentence that completes: "Unlike [competitor], we are the only app
    that ___" — it must be specific, provable, and meaningful to a 28-year-old who goes to
    the gym 3x/week. Vague claims ("smarter AI") will not convert.

[FAIL] AI visibility comparison done
  → Next step: Query "AI workout app" in ChatGPT and Perplexity and note which competitors
    are cited; competitors cited by AI tools get free discovery from the ~33% of users who
    start product searches with AI — you need to close that gap.

[FAIL] Citation sentiment analyzed
  → Next step: Check whether AI tools describe competitors positively or neutrally — if Fitbod
    is cited as "the best AI workout planner," that is the benchmark your content must surpass
    to displace it in AI citations.
```

**Section score: 0/5**

---

## Section 7 — Social Media & Launch Prep

```
[FAIL] Primary platform profiles created (TikTok, Instagram, YouTube)
  → Next step: For casual gym-goers 25-40, TikTok and Instagram Reels are your highest-ROI
    channels — create branded accounts today with consistent handle, bio, and link-in-bio
    pointing to your waitlist or app store listing.

[FAIL] X / Twitter presence established
  → Next step: Create and pin a thread explaining the app's AI approach — this is your
    credibility signal for tech-adjacent gym users and journalists who will research you.

[FAIL] Product Hunt profile ready
  → Next step: Create your PH maker profile and claim your product — B2C fitness apps can
    gain significant launch-day installs from PH even without a massive following.

[FAIL] 400+ Product Hunt upvote supporters lined up
  → Next step: DM your beta users, personal network, and fitness communities on Reddit
    (r/Fitness, r/bodyweightfitness) asking them to follow your PH page before launch day;
    49% of PH launches fail by relying on organic PH traffic alone.

[FAIL] Launch email sequence drafted (welcome → value → upgrade)
  → Next step: Write a 5-email sequence: Day 0 welcome, Day 2 first workout nudge, Day 5
    social proof (user result), Day 10 premium feature showcase, Day 14 upgrade CTA —
    this sequence is your primary free-to-paid conversion engine.

[FAIL] 30-day content calendar ready
  → Next step: Plan 30 days of content before launch: 3x/week short-form video (workout tip,
    user transformation, AI feature demo), 1x/week longer educational post — batch-produce
    the first 2 weeks before launch day so you are not creating under pressure.

[FAIL] "Magic Triangle" strategy ready (founder content + direct outreach + retargeting)
  → Next step: Set up the triangle: (1) founder posts workout content personally with app
    shown naturally, (2) DM engaged followers of Fitbod/MyFitnessPal with a specific beta
    invite, (3) install Meta Pixel now so your retargeting audience builds before you spend
    on ads.
```

**Section score: 0/7** — B2C fitness lives and dies on social proof and community. This section needs the most pre-launch investment.

---

## Section 8 — Legal & Compliance

```
[FAIL] Privacy Policy live
  → Next step: Generate a compliant Privacy Policy at Termly or Iubenda — for a fitness app
    collecting health-adjacent data (workout history, body metrics), you must explicitly disclose
    what health data is collected and how it is used; CCPA and GDPR require this before any
    public traffic.

[FAIL] Terms of Service live
  → Next step: Publish ToS before launch — include clauses for acceptable use, AI-generated
    content limitations (workouts are not medical advice), subscription terms, and refund policy.

[FAIL] Cookie consent banner implemented
  → Next step: Add a GDPR-compliant cookie banner before launch — if you run Meta Pixel or
    Google Analytics (you should), this is legally required for EU users and is a $20M GDPR
    fine risk if skipped.

[N/A] DPA template
  → Not applicable — B2C consumer product, no enterprise data processing agreements needed.
```

**Section score: 0/3** — Hard blocker. A fitness app collecting health-adjacent data without a Privacy Policy is a legal liability from day one.

---

## Section 9 — Trust Infrastructure

```
[FAIL] Auth system production-ready
  → Next step: Confirm your auth provider (Clerk, Supabase Auth, Firebase Auth, etc.) is on
    a paid plan with SLA — free-tier auth systems have rate limits that will cause sign-in
    failures during a launch spike.

[FAIL] Audit logging enabled
  → Next step: Log all auth events (sign-in, sign-out, password reset), subscription state
    changes, and AI request volume per user — you need this to debug launch-day issues and
    to detect abuse patterns.

[FAIL] Error tracking configured (Sentry or equivalent)
  → Next step: Install Sentry with source maps before launch — you will encounter errors
    during launch that you cannot reproduce locally; without error tracking you are flying blind.

[FAIL] Uptime monitoring active
  → Next step: Set up BetterUptime or UptimeRobot with a public status page and SMS/Slack
    alerts — your first 1000 users will check your status page when something breaks.

[FAIL] Security headers configured
  → Next step: Cannot verify (no live URL) — before launch, run securityheaders.com and
    ensure you have Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and HSTS configured;
    a missing CSP is a top-10 OWASP vulnerability.
```

**Section score: 0/5**

---

## Section 10 — Launch Day Readiness

```
[FAIL] Support channel operational
  → Next step: Set up Intercom or Crisp with a fitness-specific welcome message and FAQ
    pre-loaded — expect 15-20% of first-day sign-ups to send at least one support message;
    slow responses in the first 48 hours kill word-of-mouth.

[FAIL] FAQ / Knowledge base published
  → Next step: Write and publish at minimum 10 FAQ articles before launch: how AI works,
    data privacy, how to cancel, what "premium" unlocks, equipment-free options — these
    reduce support volume and improve AI citation potential simultaneously.

[FAIL] Analytics tracking verified
  → Next step: Install PostHog (recommended for B2C product analytics) or GA4 and verify
    sign-up, first workout completion, and upgrade events are firing in the debug view
    before launch — you need day-one funnel data.

[FAIL] Team roles assigned for launch day
  → Next step: Assign explicit owners for: social monitoring (respond to every mention in
    first 48 hours), support inbox, bug escalation, and PH comment responses — document
    this in a shared doc before the day.

[FAIL] Rollback plan documented
  → Next step: Write a one-page runbook: what breaks → who is paged → how to roll back
    the last deploy → how to communicate outages to users; test the rollback deploy once
    in staging before launch.
```

**Section score: 0/5**

---

## Summary Scorecard

| # | Section | Score | Status |
|---|---------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Pre-Launch Validation | 0/4 | CRITICAL |
| 2 | Pricing Strategy | 1/5 | CRITICAL |
| 3 | Payment Integration | 0/5 | HARD BLOCKER |
| 4 | AI Economics | 3/5 | GOOD — minor fixes |
| 5 | GEO & AI Discovery | 0/6 | HIGH PRIORITY |
| 6 | Competitor Intelligence | 0/5 | HIGH PRIORITY |
| 7 | Social Media & Launch | 0/7 | CRITICAL for B2C |
| 8 | Legal & Compliance | 0/3 | HARD BLOCKER |
| 9 | Trust Infrastructure | 0/5 | HARD BLOCKER |
| 10 | Launch Day Readiness | 0/5 | HIGH PRIORITY |
| **Total** | | **4/50** | **Not launch-ready** |

---

## Prioritized Fix Order

**Week 1 — Hard blockers (cannot launch without these):**
1. Submit Stripe verification + configure webhooks
2. Publish Privacy Policy and Terms of Service
3. Add cookie consent banner
4. Set up Sentry + uptime monitoring
5. Confirm auth provider is on a paid plan

**Week 2 — Revenue and conversion:**
6. Add annual billing option ($79.99/yr)
7. Add a third pricing tier
8. Write and publish pricing page
9. Write 5-email welcome sequence
10. Add usage cap logic (5 req/mo free, 50 req/mo premium)

**Week 3 — Discovery and social:**
11. Create TikTok, Instagram, X accounts
12. Build 30-day content calendar and batch-produce first 2 weeks
13. Set up Meta Pixel for retargeting
14. Set up PH profile and begin DM outreach for supporters
15. Run GEO audit (query ChatGPT/Perplexity for your category)

**Week 4 — Polish and launch:**
16. Implement FAQ schema markup
17. Verify SSR/pre-rendering for public pages
18. Publish FAQ/knowledge base (10 articles minimum)
19. Assign team launch-day roles
20. Write and test rollback runbook

---

**One verdict:** Your AI unit economics are genuinely excellent (2% of revenue is a green flag). Everything else is pre-launch work. Plan for 4-6 weeks of focused prep before you can responsibly send public traffic.
